kernel_variant.question:
Let G be a group with binary operation *. Assume
(i) G \subseteq  \mathbb{R}^3 (no relation between * and the usual vector addition is assumed);
(ii) for every u , v \in  G we have  u\times v = u*v  or  u\times v = 0  (or both), where \times  denotes the usual cross-product in \mathbb{R}^3.

Prove that u\times v = 0 for every pair u , v \in  G.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, \times  is the usual cross-product in \mathbb{R}^3, * is the group operation in G and e denotes the identity element of G.

Lemma 1.  If x , y \in  G are non-zero and x\cdot y = 0, then x*x is either the zero vector or it is parallel to y.

Proof.  Put z := x\times y.  Because x \perp  y and both are non-zero, z \neq  0; hypothesis (ii) gives
                 z = x*y.                                                    (1)
Set
                 s := (x*x)*y.                                               (2)
By associativity and (1),
                 s = x*(x*y) = x*z.                                          (3)
By (ii), x*z is either 0 or x\times z.  A triple-product computation shows
                 x\times z = x\times (x\times y) = -\|x\|^2 y,                                   (4)
so x\times z (if non-zero) is parallel to y; 0 is of course also parallel to y. Hence s is parallel to y.  Now apply (ii) to the pair (x*x , y).  Either (x*x)\times y = 0 or
                 (x*x)\times y = (x*x)*y = s.                                     (5)
If (x*x)\times y \neq  0, then (5) would say that a non-zero cross-product is parallel to y, impossible because every non-zero cross-product is perpendicular to its two factors.  Therefore (x*x)\times y = 0, so x*x is 0 or parallel to y. \blacksquare 

Lemma 2.  If x \in  G\{0} admits a non-zero y \in  G with x\cdot y = 0, then x*x = 0.

Proof.  Lemma 1 applied to (x , y) tells us that x*x is 0 or parallel to y.  Apply Lemma 1 again to (x , x\times y) (note that x\times y \neq  0 because x and y are non-zero and orthogonal).  Now x*x is 0 or parallel to x\times y.  Because y and x\times y are not parallel, the only possibility is x*x = 0. \blacksquare 

Lemma 3.  If two elements x , y \in  G commute, then x\times y = 0.

Proof.  Assume x\times y \neq  0.  Then by (ii) we have x*y = x\times y and y*x = y\times x = -x\times y \neq  x*y, contradicting xy = yx.  Hence x\times y = 0. \blacksquare 

Main proof.  Suppose, toward a contradiction, that there exist a , b \in  G with a\times b \neq  0.  Put
                 c := a\times b = a*b.                                             (6)
The vector c is orthogonal to both a and b and is non-zero.  Therefore {a , b , c} is a linearly independent set of three vectors in \mathbb{R}^3 and hence a basis of \mathbb{R}^3.

Step 1.  The identity element is the zero vector.
Because e commutes with every element of G, Lemma 3 yields
                 e\times a = e\times b = e\times c = 0.                                       (7)
Since a , b , c span \mathbb{R}^3, equation (7) forces e\times v = 0 for every v \in  \mathbb{R}^3.  The only vector whose cross-product with every vector is zero is the zero vector itself, so e = 0.                         (8)

Step 2.  Some squares are zero.
The vectors b , c and b\times c are mutually orthogonal and non-zero; moreover b\times c \neq  0 implies, by (ii),
                 b\times c = b*c \in  G.                                              (9)
Applying Lemma 2 to the orthogonal pairs (b , c), (c , b\times c) and (b*c , b) we get
                 b*b = 0,      c*c = 0,      (b*c)*(b*c) = 0.                (10)
Because e = 0, (10) reads
                 b^2 = c^2 = (b*c)^2 = e.                                      (11)
In particular, b and c are their own inverses.

Step 3.  The elements b and c commute --- contradiction.
From (11) we have b^{-1} = b and c^{-1} = c, so
                 (b*c)^{-1} = c^{-1} b^{-1} = c*b.                                   (12)
But (b*c)^2 = e implies (b*c)^{-1} = b*c, whence b*c = c*b.  Lemma 3 then forces b\times c = 0.  This contradicts the fact that b\times c \neq  0 (because b \perp  c and both are non-zero).

The contradiction shows that our initial assumption a\times b \neq  0 is impossible.  Therefore u\times v = 0 for all u , v \in  G. \blacksquare

core_steps: Orthogonal-pair lemma: for non-zero x ⟂ y, the element x*x is forced to be parallel to y (via the cross-product alternatives).
Zero-square lemma: if some non-zero y ⟂ x exists, then x*x must actually be 0.
Commutativity lemma: if two group elements commute, their cross product must vanish.
Contradiction setup: pick a,b with a×b ≠ 0, set c = a×b = a*b; the identity e commutes with a,b,c, so Lemma 3 gives e×v = 0 for every v ⇒ e = 0.
Final clash: Lemma 2 forces b*b = c*c = 0, so b*c = c*b, violating Lemma 3; hence no initial pair with non-zero cross product can exist.

slot1.description: Symbol chosen for the group operation.

slot2.description: Letters used to denote the three key non-zero vectors employed in the contradiction (currently a, b, c).

slot3.description: Letter used for the group identity element.

slot4.description: Explicit division of the argument into exactly three separately-named lemmas (they could be merged or renumbered without affecting the logic).
